1.1.section: 1. Data description and collection or re-use of existing data
1.1.question: How will new data be collected or produced and/or how will existing data be re-used?
1.1.content:
Experimental data will be generated during synthesis, physicochemical characterization, and
optical investigation of azo -functionalized MOF materials and receptor -layer systems. The
project will involve acquisition of spectroscopic, structural, thermal, microscopic, and optical -
response datasets obtained using standard analy tical instrumentation and laboratory
software. Data processing will include interpretation of structure –property –interaction
relationships, comparative analysis of analyte -dependent optical responses, and correlation
of physicochemical parameters with sens ing behavior. Selected literature -derived and
publicly available datasets related to MOF systems and supramolecular receptor materials
may additionally be used for comparative interpretation of the obtained results.

optical investigation of azo -functionalized MOF materials and receptor -layer systems. The
project will involve acquisition of spectroscopic, structural, thermal, microscopic, and optical -
response datasets obtained using standard analy tical instrumentation and laboratory
software. Data processing will include interpretation of structure –property –interaction

1.2.section: 1. Data description and collection or re-use of existing data
1.2.question: What data (for example the types, formats, and volumes) will be collected or produced?
1.2.content:
The project will generate raw and processed experimental datasets including synthetic
protocols, NMR, FTIR, UV –Vis, fluorescence, NIR, PXRD/XRD, DSC/TGA, and SEM data, as well
as optical sensing measurements, analyte -recognition studi es, microscopy images, calibration
datasets, and comparative structure –interaction –response analyses. Data will be stored
primarily in digital form using instrument -generated formats and additionally converted
where appropriate into commonly accessible for mats such as CSV, TXT, PDF, PNG, TIFF, and

2.1.question: What metadata and documentation (for example methodology or data collection and way of organising data) will accompany data?
2.1.content:
sample identity, synthesis conditions, instrumental parameters, analyte composition,
processing methods, and experimental conditions relevant for interpretation and
reproducibility of the obtained results. Data and metadata will be organized according to
research tasks, analytical methods, and experimental series using consist ent file naming
conventions and standardized directory structures. Metadata descriptions will include sample
identifiers, experimental conditions, units, and processing parameters.
Laboratory documentation will include synthetic procedures, characterizatio n protocols,
calibration parameters, data -processing procedures, and descriptions of analytical workflows.
Where appropriate, README files and supplementary metadata descriptions will additionally
be prepared to improve interoperability and long -term usabi lity of the datasets. Metadata
and documentation will be prepared in accordance with FAIR principles to facilitate future
interpretation, accessibility, and potential reuse of research data.

2.2.question: What data quality control measures will be used?
2.2.content:
Data quality will be ens ured through application of standard quality -control procedures
routinely used in synthetic chemistry, materials characterization, and spectroscopic analysis.
Experimental results will be verified using replicate measurements, comparison of
complementary a nalytical techniques, calibration of analytical instrumentation, and
validation of reproducibility of selected synthesis and characterization procedures.
The reliability of the obtained datasets will additionally be assessed through comparative
analysis of physicochemical, structural, thermal, and optical measurements performed for
independent samples and experimental series. Raw and processed datasets will be
periodically reviewed by project participants in order to identify inconsistencies, experimental
artefacts, or data -processing errors.

3.1.section: 3. Storage and backup during the research process
3.1.question: How will data and metadata be stored and backed up during the research process?
3.1.content:
Research data and accompanying metadata generated during the project realization will be
stored on secured institutional infrastructure available at Gdańsk University of Technology
and collaborating research institutions. Working datasets will additionally be stored on
password -protected laboratory workstations and secured external st orage devices accessible
only to authorized project participants.
Backup procedures will include regular creation of backup copies of raw and processed
datasets, metadata files, laboratory documentation, and processed analytical results. Backup
copies will be stored in separate locations in accordance with institutional recommendations
and good research -data -management practices. Whenever technically feasible, the project
will follow the recommended 3 -2-1 backup strategy involving multiple copies of dataset s
stored on different storage media.
Raw instrumental data, processed datasets, and metadata will be preserved throughout the
entire project duration in order to ensure reproducibility, long -term accessibility, and
integrity of the obtained experimental re sults.

3.2.section: 3. Storage and backup during the research process
3.2.question: How will data security and protection of sensitive data be taken care of during the research?
3.2.content:
The project does not involve collection or processing of personal, medical, or otherwise
sensitive data. The generated datasets will consist exclusively of experim ental research data
associated with synthesis, physicochemical characterization, and optical investigation of
functional materials.
Access to raw and processed datasets during the project realization will be restricted to
authorized project participants an d collaborating researchers directly involved in data
analysis and interpretation. Data -management procedures applied within the project will
comply with institutional regulations concerning research -data security, intellectual -property
protection, and goo d scientific practice. Temporary restrictions on access to unpublished

4.2.question: How will other legal issues, such as intellectual property rights and ownership, be managed? What legislation is applicable?
4.2.content:
Research data management and dissemination procedures applied within the project will
comply with institutional regulations concerning intellectual -property protection, ownership
of research results, and principles of good scientific practice. Ownership of research data,
laboratory documentation, and generated intellectual property will follow the regulations
and policies applicable at Gdańsk University of Technology and Medical University of Gdańsk .
Unpublished data sets, raw analytical results, and potentially implementation -relevant
findings may remain temporarily restricted until completion of publication procedures,
intellectual -property evaluation, or other dissemination activities. Data underlying published
scientific results will be shared in accordance with FAIR principles, open -science
recommendations, and publisher requirements whenever possible.
Where appropriate, selected datasets and supplementary materials associated with scientific
publications may be de posited in trusted repositories with assignment of persistent
identifiers (DOI) and appropriate metadata descriptions.

identifiers (DOI) and appropriate metadata descriptions.

5.1.question: How and when will data be shared? Are there possible restrictions to data sharing or embargo reasons?
5.1.content:
lub przyczyny embarga
Data underlying scientific publications will be shared whenever possible in accordance with
FAIR principles, NCN recommendations, and publisher requirements. Selected datasets and
supplementary materials will be deposited in trusted open -access repositories, preferably
MOST Danych repository of Gdańsk University of Technology, after publication of the
corresponding results. Temporary embargo periods may be applied in cases related to
ongoing publications or intellectual -property evalu ation.

5.2.question: How will data for preservation be selected, and where will data be preserved long-term (for example a data repository or archive)?
5.2.content:
Long -term preservation will concern datasets directly associated with scientific publications,
including spec troscopic, diffraction, thermal -analysis, microscopy, and optical -response data
together with metadata and supplementary documentation. Data will be archived using
institutional infrastructure and trusted repositories, including MOST Danych repository, for a
minimum period of 10 years.

together with metadata and supplementary documentation. Data will be archived using
institutional infrastructure and trusted repositories, including MOST Danych repository, for a
minimum period of 10 years.

5.3.question: What methods or software tools will be needed to access and use the data?
5.3.content:
Research data will be stored using standard instrument -generated formats and additionally
converted where appropriate into accessible forma ts such as CSV, TXT, PDF, PNG, TIFF, and
XLSX to facilitate interoperability and reuse using standard scientific and office software.

tagged_paragraphs.text: Research data will be stored using standard instrument -generated formats and additionally
converted where appropriate into accessible forma ts such as CSV, TXT, PDF, PNG, TIFF, and
XLSX to facilitate interoperability and reuse using standard scientific and office software.

5.4.question: How will the application of a unique and persistent identifier (such as a Digital Object Identifier (DOI)) to each data set be ensured?
5.4.content:
Datasets deposited in open repositories will receive persistent identifiers such as DOI
numbers assigned by the hosting repository. Deposited datasets will additionally be
accompanied by metadata and README documentation enabling identification, citation, and
long -term accessibility of the research data.

6.1.question: Who (for example role, position, and institution) will be responsible for data management (i.e. the data steward)?
6.1.content:
Responsibility for research data management within the project will be coordinated by Dr.
Eng. Michał Nowak (Gdańsk University of Technology), who will serve as the project data
steward. Responsibilities will include supervision of data organization, metadata preparation,
backup procedure s, quality -control verification, and coordination of long -term archiving and
repository deposition of selected datasets associated with scientific publications. Quarterly
internal reviews of data organization, accessibility, metadata completeness, and back up
integrity will additionally be performed in order to ensure compliance with FAIR principles
and institutional data -management recommendations. Individual project participants will be
responsible for proper documentation and organization of datasets gene rated within their
respective research tasks.

6.2.question: What resources (for example financial and time) will be dedicated to data management and ensuring the data will be FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Re-usable)?
6.2.content:
Research data management will be performed using institutional infrastructure and standard
software tools routinely available at participating institutions. Time and organizational
resources necessary for metadata preparation, data organization, backup creation, repository
deposition, and long -term archiving will be incorporated into routine project activities.
Standardized file naming conventions, metadata descriptions, README documentation, and
use of accessible data formats will support findability, accessibility, interoperability, and
future re use of selected datasets in accordance with FAIR principles.
